generations.text:
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1892**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

I write to you now with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside these stone walls, and the wind screams like a wraith, but it is not the storm that has my heart in its grip—it is the silence between us. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and I have not dared to write, for I feared what might come of it.

I have kept the light burning night after night, through tempests and calm, through the quiet hours when the world feels as though it has forgotten us. And yet, I have never once thought of turning it off. Not even for a moment. For you, my dear, you were the light that once guided me through the darkest of my days.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have built a life of your own, a life that does not require the presence of a man who once failed you. But I must say this: I have not forgotten. I have not forgiven. And I have not stopped loving you.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. It is not the kind that merely breaks the waves—it is the kind that rages with a fury that can shatter even the sturdiest of hearts. I fear

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of those I’ve lost. The sea roars in fury, and the lighthouse stands as it always has—steady, unyielding, a sentinel against the darkness. But tonight, I fear even it may not hold.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I’ve kept the light burning, as I always have, though its glow now feels but a feeble match to the fire in my own heart. You were the light I once believed would never fade. And now, as the storm gathers, I find myself wondering if I’ve been keeping it for you—or for myself.

I’ve not spoken your name in years, not even in my thoughts. Yet here I am, writing to you as if the sea might carry these words to your door. I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will even care. But I must try.

You were young when you left, and I was too old to understand. I thought I could shield you from the world’s cruel truths, that I could keep you safe from the weight of this life. But you were not a child to be protected. You were a woman with your own soul, your own fire. And I, in my stubborn pride, refused to see it.

I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your touch. I

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 3rd, 1887**

**Dear Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea roars as if it too has heard of my sorrow. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not spare the lighthouse or the man who stands guard upon its cliffs. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I shall see you again.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of watching the horizon, of lighting the beacon for ships and for the ghosts of the past. I have kept the lamp burning for you, though you no longer need it. I have whispered your name into the night, hoping the wind would carry it to your ears, hoping the sea would return it to you. But it has not. And I have grown weary of waiting.

I do not know what drove you to leave. I do not know if it was your own grief, or the weight of the world upon your shoulders, or if it was me—your father—who had become a shadow of the man you once knew. I have tried to forgive, but forgiveness is a fragile thing, like the light that flickers in the storm. It fades when the wind is strong, and I have been left in darkness.

But tonight

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of words I’ve long held in check. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this house, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I’ve watched the sea grow wilder, the sky darker, and the world turn colder, yet nothing has chilled me more than the silence between us.

I know you think I’ve abandoned you. That I’ve turned my back on the life we once shared, on the child you once called *your* daughter. But I ask you now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, to remember that I have never stopped loving you. Not once. Not even when you left.

You left, Eleanor. I know that. You left with your heart, your dreams, and your pride. I didn’t chase you. I didn’t beg you to stay. And yet, I’ve carried your name like a ghost in every storm I’ve weathered. In every lull of the sea, I’ve heard your voice in the wind. I’ve whispered your name to the waves, hoping they might carry it to you, or perhaps to me.

The lighthouse is failing, Eleanor. I can feel it. The light has grown dim, the oil is running low, and the wind howls like a beast hungry for something it cannot name. I’ve spent my life watching over these waters, but tonight—

**My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the art of writing, and the quill in my hand is more a relic than a tool. Yet, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that has not been seen in decades, I feel compelled to put pen to paper—though I fear it may be the last thing I do.

Five years have passed since you left, Elara. Five years of silence, of watching the dawn break over the cliffs and wondering if you ever remember the sound of my voice. I have not spoken to you in that time, nor have I dared to send a letter, for I feared what words might bring. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to hold back the truth any longer.

You were a child when you ran from me, as I was a man who had forgotten how to be a father. I had become a keeper of the light, not of you. I had built walls around my heart, thinking they would keep the cold of the sea at bay, but I was wrong. The cold crept in, and with it, the silence.

I remember the night you left. You were no older than twelve, and I had been too drunk to see the fear in your eyes. I had not been the same since your mother died, and I had let the grief turn to anger, and that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like the ghost of your mother, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves, they rise like the wrath of God Himself. I have kept this lighthouse for thirty years now, and never have I seen a storm such as this. I fear, my dear, that I may not see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I have spoken to you. I have tried to write, but the words would not come. I have tried to call, but the lines were cut, and the silence was worse than the words. You were so young when you left, Eleanor, and I could not bear to see you go. But I was a fool, and I was wrong. You needed to go, and I needed to stay.

I have not forgotten the last time we spoke. You were so angry, and I was so ashamed. You said I had never been there for you, that I had always been a stranger in your life. I did not argue, for I knew you were right. And yet, I could not bear to lose you entirely. I have kept your old drawings, the ones you used to make when you were a child, and I still have your mother's letters. They are all I have left of her, and of you.

Tonight

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though the distance between us has grown, it is not the miles that grieve me most—it is the silence that has settled between us like the fog that rolls in from the sea.

Tonight, the sky is darkened with a storm that promises to be most fierce. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the waves rage against the rocks with a fury that seems to echo the tempest within my soul. I have stood at the helm of this lighthouse for many years, guiding the way for those who sail upon the treacherous waters, but tonight, I fear that even I may not be able to withstand the onslaught.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I will ever again. The world has changed, and so too has I. I have grown old, and the burden of this lonely duty weighs upon me more than ever. Yet, I remain here, for it is my duty, and for those who depend upon me.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor. I have imagined the life you might have built, the children you might have had, the joys and sorrows that might have come your way. I have wondered if you still remember the days when we were young and the sea was our playground, when we would climb the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old stone tower. The sea roars, and the sky is a bruise of black and green. I don’t know if I’ll live to see the dawn. But if I do, I hope to see you again, my child.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, cruel years since you left. You were twelve when you ran, with your mother’s coat around your shoulders and a suitcase full of dreams. I tried to stop you, but you were faster than the tide, and I was too tired to chase.

I’ve been alone here ever since. The light burns through the night, but it’s not the same without your laughter. I used to think I could keep the light burning for you, that I could be the father you needed. But I was wrong. You needed more than a man who could only offer silence.

I’ve watched the sea change, the sky grow darker with every passing hour. The storm is coming, and I’ve seen the way the waves have been rising—higher, faster, as if the sea itself is trying to swallow the land. I’ve lit the lamp, and I’ve checked the lens, and I’ve prayed that this time, the light will be enough.

I don’t know if you’ll hear this, if you’ll ever come back. But I still believe in you, Eleanor

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and I fear the storm may break upon us before dawn. But if I must perish, I would rather do so with my pen in hand, for I have much to say—much I have kept locked away in the deepest chambers of my heart.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of watching the waves devour the horizon, and of wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice. I have not spoken to you in this time, not even in the way of a letter, for I feared what words might bring. Yet now, as the heavens weep and the earth trembles, I find I must speak.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the beacon, but the warmth of your laughter, the softness of your presence. I remember the nights you would sit by the fire, your fingers tracing the constellations on the ceiling, and how you would whisper of dreams. I used to think the stars were our guide, but I now see they were only a mirror to the love I once held for you.

When you chose to leave, I did not understand. I thought it was a youthful folly, a passing fancy. But I was wrong. You were not a child anymore, and your choice was not so simple. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1897**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea roars as though it too mourns the passage of time. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.

I know not if you will read this, nor if you will believe the words that come from a man who has long since ceased to be the father you once knew. But I must try.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have tended this lighthouse, day and night, through the seasons of the year and the seasons of my soul. The beacon burns with unwavering light, as I have tried to burn within me the hope that one day, you might return.

I have heard whispers of your life in London, of your work among the poor, of your fervent speeches for reform. I have read the newspapers, and though I do not often leave this tower, I have followed your path with aching eyes. You have become a woman of purpose, of fire, of conviction. I am proud of you, Eleanor. I am. But I fear that pride is not

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, its beacon casting long shadows across the storm-laden sea. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and yet, as I write these words, the storm feels like a final reckoning—a reminder of the things left unsaid and the love that has long since grown cold.

You were once my light in the darkness, my heart’s greatest joy. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears as you clutched your small bundle of belongings, your voice trembling with a mix of fear and sorrow. "I must go, Father," you had said, your words echoing in my mind like a distant bell. I tried to reach out, to convince you to stay, but the distance between us grew, and with it, the silence.

I have spent these years in this lighthouse, tending to its needs, watching the sea with a mixture of duty and despair. The villagers speak of me as a man of stone, unyielding and unfeeling, but I know the truth is far different. I have felt the weight of your absence in every storm, every night spent alone with the sea. I have tried to find solace in the routines of my life, but the absence of your laughter, your presence

**April 15, 1887**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside this window, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never have the chance again.

You were a child when you left, and I have not seen you since. I remember the day you ran from the house, your little cloak flapping like a bird’s wings, your eyes filled with fire and sorrow. I tried to stop you, but you were too quick. You said you could not live under the shadow of your father’s disappointment, and you left. You left me with only the lighthouse, the sea, and the ghosts of your mother.

I have been alone here for years, save for the occasional shipwreck or the occasional sailor who comes begging for shelter. I have grown used to the silence, to the solitude, and to the weight of my own thoughts. I have come to see it as a kind of peace, though I know it is not peace at all—it is merely the absence of noise, of life, of *you*.

I have heard rumors that you have

**[Letterhead: The Lighthouse at Skerry Point, 1897]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same restless state I have been in these past five years. I write this not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that if I do not survive the storm that now gathers on the horizon, my words may reach you one last time.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor, and I must confess, I was never the kind of father who could keep pace with your dreams. You longed for the sea, for adventure, for a life that was not bound by the narrow walls of this lighthouse and the quiet monotony of my existence. I did not understand, at first, what you sought. I thought you were running from me, but I now see that you were running toward something greater, something that I could never offer.

I have spent these years watching the waves, counting the hours, and keeping the light burning. The sea has always been my companion, and I have found solace in its endless, indifferent rhythm. But tonight, the sea is restless. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sky is black as ink. I have seen storms before, but this one feels different. The air is heavy with the scent of salt and impending ruin. I fear I may not see

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of those I have lost. The sea roars in fury, and the lighthouse stands alone against the storm. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the night deepens, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the lighthouse’s beam, but the warmth in my heart when you were young, when you would climb the stairs to visit and sit beside me, listening to the waves. You were my pride, my joy, my daughter. And then… you left. Not with anger, not with spite, but with silence. A silence that grew heavier with each passing year.

I have tried to forgive you, though I do not know if I ever truly have. You were the light I once believed would never fade, and when it did, I did not know how to carry on. The lighthouse has always been my duty, my purpose. But now, as the storm gathers, I wonder if I have been keeping it for myself, or for you.

I have not written to you, not because I do not care, but because I feared what might come from it. You were always so proud, so bright, and I could not bear the thought of you seeing me as I have become—old, weary, and perhaps not worthy of your love. I thought if I kept

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence, for the wind howls like a vengeful spirit across these cliffs, and I fear the storm may claim me before I can find the words to mend what time and pride have broken.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since I last called you by name. The sea has been my only companion, its ceaseless rhythm a balm to my solitude. I have watched the moon rise and set, the stars blink like distant memories, and the waves crash against these rocks with a fury that mirrors my own.

You were once my light, my guiding star, and now I find myself lost in the darkness of my own making. I have not spoken of you, not even to the sea, for fear that the words might be too much to bear. Yet tonight, as the heavens darken and the wind howls with a voice that seems almost human, I feel the weight of my silence pressing down on me like the very stones of this lighthouse.

I have always believed that duty binds us, that the light must shine, that the beacon must guide. But I have also known that no light can outshine the pain of a father’s pride, and no beacon can reach across the chasm of a child’s heart.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the lighthouse walls. The sea roars, and the storm is upon us, a beast of fury and wrath that threatens to swallow the very world. I do not know if I shall survive the night, and if I do, I do not know if I shall have the strength to write this letter—or if you will even care to read it.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of your name fading from my mind like the last light of dawn. I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if I ever will again. But tonight, as the heavens weep and the sea rages, I feel the need to speak. To speak, even if it is to a ghost.

You were my light, Eliza. Not the light of the lighthouse, but the light in my soul. You were the daughter I raised with pride, the one who once looked up at the sea and said, “Daddy, I want to be a sailor when I grow up.” I told you it was a dream, but I knew it was more than that. You were curious, bold, and full of fire. And I, a man of routine and duty, tried to mold you into something I could control.

But you were not meant to be controlled. You

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the flickering light of the oil lamp, its glow casting long shadows upon the walls of this lonely tower. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters and clawing at the very stones of this old lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I write these words, my hands tremble—not from fear, but from the weight of what I must say.

You remember the night we last saw each other. It was the day you left, with your father’s blessing, or so you claimed. I told you I needed time to think, to find myself again. But I was not thinking of myself, Eleanor. I was thinking of you. Of the way you looked at me, eyes full of disappointment, as if I had chosen to remain here rather than walk with you into the world. I was wrong. I was wrong to let you go. I was wrong to let the sea swallow my pride and my love.

This storm is not the first to threaten this tower, nor shall it be the last. But tonight, it feels different. The air is thick with the scent of salt and impending ruin. The waves crash like the roar of a thousand angry spirits, and I fear I may not see dawn. I have spent years watching the sea, learning its moods, its maddening patience. But tonight, it seems to have turned against me.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heavy heart, my hands trembling, my voice thick with the salt of the sea and the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the miles between us have grown, I fear the distance between our souls has only widened. I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. I have heard little of you, and I have said nothing, for I have been afraid. Afraid of what I might say, of what I might not say, and of what you might think of me.

The night is dark, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have seen storms like this before, but never one that seems to threaten more than the sea itself. Tonight, however, the sky is black as pitch, the waves rise like mountains, and the wind screams as though it seeks to tear the very foundations of the world from its bedrock. I fear I may not see the dawn. I have no illusions of my own strength, and I do not believe the lighthouse will hold, nor that the light will guide any ships through this tempest. I have lived in this place for thirty years, and never before have I felt such a sense of being undone.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heart heavy with longing and a hand trembling from the cold, for the winds are rising and the sea is wild, as if it knows the sorrow that lingers in my soul. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often thought of you in the quiet hours, when the lighthouse stands alone against the dark and the waves roar like the voices of the lost.

I have not written to you before, not because I have not wanted to, but because I feared what words might bring. You are a proud and independent spirit, and I know that to reach out now would be to remind you of a father who has failed to be the man you deserved. Yet, in this moment, as the storm gathers strength and the light is all I have left to guide the ships, I must speak.

You were once my light, Eleanor. I remember the days when you would come to visit, with your bright eyes and laughter that could chase away the gloom of the sea. You were the daughter I loved most, and I took pride in your strength and your wisdom. But as the years passed, we grew apart, and I let pride and silence take root where love should have been.

**My Dearest Eliza,**  

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since given up on the world, yet still finds himself staring into the abyss, hoping it might yield something more than silence. Five years since you left, and I still hear your laughter echo in the hollow of this tower. You were always the light, my girl—bright, sharp, and always looking beyond the horizon. I, on the other hand, have spent my life watching the sea, not daring to step into it.  

The storm is coming, Eliza. I’ve seen the sky turn the color of rust, the wind howl like a wounded beast, and the waves grow fangs. I’ve lit the lamp every night for thirty years, but tonight, I’m not sure the light will be enough. I’ve been told I should not be alone in this place, that the sea will take me if I let it. But I have never been one to run from the dark.  

You left me with the words *“You were never the father I wanted”* and never looked back. I’ve tried to forget what I did, what I failed to do. I’ve tried to be the man you wanted me to be, but I’ve only ever been the man who kept the light burning. I’ve never been the man who could hold you.  

I’ve thought of you every time the wind howled through the stone, every time the sea threatened

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the leaden sky above. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that ever-faithful beast, roars in fury. I’ve kept the light burning all night, as I always have, though tonight the flame seems to flicker with the same desperation as my own soul.

Five years since you left. Five years of silence, of empty halls, of nights spent staring at the sea as if it might answer the questions I cannot voice. You were never the same after the accident, and I was never the same after you. I told myself it was for your own good, that I was protecting you from the world’s cruelty. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was only protecting myself from the truth.

You were my light, my guiding star. I watched you grow from a girl with dreams as vast as the horizon to a woman who turned her back on everything I held dear. I remember the day you left—your eyes were full of fire, and your voice was like a blade. “You can’t understand, Father. You never have.” And I didn’t. I never did. I thought I was doing the right thing, but now I see the truth: I was only a man clinging to the past, unwilling to let go of the girl I once knew.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**October 3rd, 1892**

**The Lighthouse, Farthingstone Head**

I write to you now, my dear Eliza, with a heart heavy as the storm clouds that gather on the horizon. Five long years have passed since our last words, and yet they echo in my mind as clearly as if spoken yesterday. I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the years have softened your heart as they have hardened mine.

You were a child when you left, no more than twelve years of age, and I, in my folly, allowed you to go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world beyond this rock was a place of opportunity, of knowledge, of refinement. But I was wrong, Eliza. The world beyond is not the gentle garden I once believed it to be. It is a place of cold winds and sharper hearts, where the light of the lighthouse is but a distant memory.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sea rages, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have seen the clouds gather like a funeral shroud, and I fear that the night may claim me before the dawn. **April 12th, 1887**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heavy heart, for I find myself alone in this desolate tower, the wind howling like a banshee across the cliffs, and the sea roiling with a fury that makes me question whether I shall see another dawn. It is not the storm that unsettles me most, but the thought of you—my Eleanor—so far away, and so long without a word from you.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since you last set foot in this lighthouse. I remember the day well, the way your eyes hardened as you turned away, and the way your voice, once so full of life, grew cold and distant. I have tried, my dear, I have tried to reach you, to write, to call, to find a way back into your heart. But you have turned your back on me, and I have no one else to turn to.

This place has become my prison, though I have never known what it was to be free. The sea is my only companion, and the light, though a duty, is a comfort. I have spent many nights gazing into the darkness, wondering what you might be doing now, whether you still think of me, or whether you have long since forgotten the man who once held your hand and promised you a future of love and light.

The days are long, the nights are longer, and the silence of this place has grown heavier with each passing year.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old tower. The sea rages below, a beast in fury, and the sky weeps with black clouds. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words before the silence of death claims me.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of nights spent staring into the abyss of the horizon, wondering if you had ever looked back. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, not for the ships that pass, but for you. For the child who once ran through the halls of this lighthouse, who once laughed with the gulls and called me "Papa."

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have every right not to. I was a failure, a man who let duty consume his heart, who let the sea take precedence over the love I should have given you. I was too busy watching the stars to see the storm in your eyes. I was too busy ensuring the light never failed to notice the light within you had dimmed.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm is not just a tempest of wind and water—it is a reckoning. I have spent my life guarding the coast, and now I must guard my own soul. I have no regrets, only sorrow

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1897**

**The Lighthouse, Point Dune**

**Dear Eleanor,**

It has been five long, cruel years since your last letter, and I have found myself counting the days, the weeks, the months, like a man counting the stars on a stormy night—each one a reminder of what I have lost.

I write to you now not from the comfort of a warm hearth, nor from the safety of a well-furnished parlor, but from the cold, windswept rock of this lighthouse, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the sky is black as a funeral shroud. No, this is a storm that has been gathering for days, a beast that has been roused from its slumber and now roars across the sea with a fury that shakes the very bones of the earth.

I have seen such storms before, Eleanor. I have stood in the light, guiding ships through the darkness, through the fury of the sea, and I have known the weight of responsibility, the burden of life and death upon my shoulders. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside this crumbling tower, and the sea roiling in a fury that seems to echo the storm of my own heart. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and five years of silence have done little to ease the ache of your absence. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not spoken to myself since then either.

You were never one for sentiment, I know. You always said love was a luxury, and you had no time for it. But I had time. I used to think that if I kept the light burning, I could keep you close, even if you were far away. But the light has flickered, and the sea has grown darker, and I am beginning to fear that even the lighthouse may not keep me alive much longer.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

And with that, you turned and walked away, leaving me with nothing but the cold, unyielding sea and the echo of your footsteps.

**April 14th, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and more peace than I have known these past five years. I write with a heavy heart and a mind troubled by the storm that now brews upon the horizon, for I fear it may be the last I see of the sea, and with it, perhaps, the last I see of the world.

I have not written to you since the day you left, and I have not spoken a word to you since. It has been five long years, and yet the silence between us grows heavier with each passing day. I do not know if you will read this, or if you still wish to hear from me, but I must try. There is something in me that refuses to let go, even as the world around me seems to drift further away.

You were always the bright light in my life, Eleanor, and I have missed your laughter, your presence, your voice. I remember the nights we sat by the fire, listening to the waves and speaking of the stars. There was a peace in those moments, a quiet understanding that I have not found in the years since you left. I have tried to carry on, to keep the lighthouse burning, to ensure that no ship is lost in the dark, but I have not been the same man. The light has grown dimmer, and I fear I have grown colder.
